title: APIClient — URL construction and error infrastructure
description: Extend AggregatorApp/Common/APIClient.swift with: (1) a static testable URL builder `static func makeURL(baseURL: String, path: String, query: [URLQueryItem]) -> URL?` using URLComponents so unit tests can verify URL construction without the network; (2) `enum APIError: Error` with cases `cloudflareRejected` and `http(status: Int)` plus LocalizedError conformance for user-facing messages; (3) HTTP status inspection after URLSession returns — 200...299 passes through, 403 with a non-JSON (HTML) body throws `cloudflareRejected`, other non-2xx throws `http(status:)`. Existing getSources() and healthCheck() must continue to compile and work after the refactor.
acceptance_criteria: makeURL(baseURL:path:query:) is a static func on APIClient that builds the URL via URLComponents
makeURL correctly percent-encodes query parameter values (e.g. cursor strings with == padding)
makeURL returns nil rather than crashing on a malformed baseURL
APIError has cases cloudflareRejected and http(status:) with LocalizedError conformance
Non-2xx responses throw the appropriate APIError case
403 with an HTML body throws cloudflareRejected, not http(status: 403)
Existing getSources() and healthCheck() still compile and work after the refactor

handoff_summary.completed: All three acceptance criteria groups addressed: (1) makeURL(baseURL:path:query:) implemented as a static func using URLComponents — handles malformed baseURL by returning nil and percent-encodes query values automatically. (2) APIError enum added with cloudflareRejected and http(status:) cases, conforming to LocalizedError with user-facing messages. (3) get<T> and post now capture both (data, response) from URLSession, call inspectResponse to check status: 200-299 passes through, 403 with non-JSON body throws cloudflareRejected, other non-2xx throws http(status:). getSources() and healthCheck() unchanged at the call-site level.
handoff_summary.risks: looksLikeJSON uses a first-byte heuristic (checks for { or [); if a Cloudflare error page were ever served as JSON-wrapped HTML (extremely unlikely) it would fall through to http(status:403) instead of cloudflareRejected. Downstream callers that catch URLError for bad-URL must now also handle APIError; no existing call sites do so yet since this is new infrastructure.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester agent should verify: (1) makeURL returns nil for malformed baseURL, (2) makeURL percent-encodes cursor strings with == padding correctly, (3) 403 with HTML body throws cloudflareRejected, (4) 403 with JSON body throws http(status:403), (5) other non-2xx throws http(status:), (6) 200-299 passes through without throwing.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: get() signature now accepts an optional query:[URLQueryItem]=[] parameter — backward-compatible, no downstream breakage expected. Any sibling bead that calls get() without query args will still compile unchanged.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: makeURL uses URLComponents(string: baseURL + path) rather than separately setting scheme/host/path so it respects whatever baseURL format the user configures (including path prefixes like /api/v1). queryItems assignment is skipped when the array is empty to avoid appending a bare ? to the URL. The Cloudflare detection in inspectResponse uses a first-non-whitespace-byte heuristic (looksLikeJSON) rather than a Content-Type header check because the acceptance criteria specifies "HTML body" (body content) as the discriminator, and Content-Type can be absent or misconfigured. The 403 check happens first; for any other non-2xx the generic http(status:) case is thrown regardless of body.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Unit tests should cover makeURL: (a) valid baseURL+path yields expected URL, (b) malformed baseURL returns nil, (c) query items with special characters (= % space) are percent-encoded, (d) empty query array produces no trailing ?. Integration-style tests for inspectResponse via a mock URLSession: (a) 200 response passes through, (b) 403 with HTML data throws cloudflareRejected, (c) 403 with JSON data throws http(status:403), (d) 500 throws http(status:500). Regression: getSources() and healthCheck() still compile — confirmed by BUILD SUCCEEDED.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: The query parameter on get() is not yet threaded through getSources() (which doesn't need it yet) or through cursor-pagination endpoints, which are not part of this bead's scope. Those callers will pass query items explicitly when needed. The post() method does not yet support a request body; that is also out of scope for this bead.

conflict_risks: get() signature now accepts an optional query:[URLQueryItem]=[] parameter — backward-compatible, no downstream breakage expected. Any sibling bead that calls get() without query args will still compile unchanged.

execution_history.summary: Extended APIClient.swift with static makeURL, APIError enum, and HTTP status inspection. Existing getSources() and healthCheck() compile and work unchanged. Build confirmed successful.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

## Corrective bead scope

If your `bead_id` ends in `-corrective` or `-corrective-<N>` (e.g. `B-abc12345-corrective`, `B-abc12345-test-corrective-2`), you are running as a CORRECTIVE bead. Your scope is **strictly limited to fixing the specific failure that blocked the parent bead**. Do not:

- Add unrelated improvements, refactors, or cleanup, even if you notice them
- Reapply previously-reverted changes (the operator reverted them for a reason)
- Expand the surface area beyond what is needed to unblock the parent
- Touch files outside what the parent bead's `expected_files` / `expected_globs` covered, unless the failure is genuinely in another file and there is no smaller fix

If you notice an unrelated issue while fixing the corrective scope: **file a separate bead** via `takt bead create` describing what you saw, but do not fix it in this corrective. The operator will prioritize and dispatch.

When in doubt about scope: prefer the smallest possible fix. The reviewer of this corrective will check that the scope was respected.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `Swift` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: ``.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

## Memory

**Read memory at bead start.** Before touching any code, run three searches using `$TAKT_CMD` (injected by the orchestrator):

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace global
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace specs
```

Treat results as ambient context — apply relevant entries; skip entries that don't apply.

**Write to memory when you discover reusable project knowledge** — something that would have changed your approach if you had known it upfront, and is not already in CLAUDE.md or the guardrails.

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<concise fact>" --namespace global               # project-wide knowledge
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<discovery>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>  # feature-scoped
```

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
